cross_cell_repeated_theses.thesis: your constant surveillance keeps you performing when you're already running on empty.
you believe your value lives in your usefulness, nowhere else.
you see now that the system you built is turning against you.
you must stop managing before you feel safe; safety comes after the stopping.
you'll manage your energy differently now; not because you've stopped caring, but because you've learned to care for yourself.
you are not the efficient, untiring thing you've been trying to be.
you can observe the urge to manage without acting on every impulse.
you release the need for perfect control and keep the ability to affect your own life.
your body is paying the price of the strategy your mind thinks is keeping you safe.
all your monitoring hasn't actually prevented the collapse you fear.
your nervous system knows the truth before your mind will admit it.
your body's depletion is not weakness; it's information about what was unsustainable.
you've been still and small to honor what was lost, and that stillness became your identity.
you believe your love is measured in how much you've been willing to forfeit.
you're aware now that you chose to stop living.
you are not an absence; you are a person who was living in one.
loss can be real without requiring you to become smaller in response.
you release the paralysis and keep the love that made you still.
you live while grief is still fresh, before it becomes safe to live.
the grief doesn't leave; you become someone who can carry it without letting it carry you.
honoring loss through absence means you're also absent from your own life.
stillness isn't honoring what was lost; it's letting loss define the rest.
the body grieves differently than the mind; it holds what can't be said.
your pain is evidence of what mattered to you; don't call it weakness.
the hypervigilance you've built is so familiar you can't feel it anymore.
you believe the only safety is total knowledge, total control.
you can no longer deny that your fear is your own creation.
you move toward what you need while the terror insists it's not safe yet.
the fear will still arrive, but you'll know it's not the truth, and that changes everything.
you are not your anxiety; you are the one anxiety is happening to.
